macro_segments.title: The Wife's Labor and Kiddushin: Ownership and Control

micro_segments.title: Introduction: Who Owns the Wife's Labor and the Case of Kiddushin
Two Foundational Approaches: Rashi vs. Tosafot on Ownership Without Refusal
The Mishnah's Case of Motar (Excess Labor) and the Debate Between Rabbi Meir and Rabbi Yochanan HaSandlar

macro_segments.title: The Amoraic Interpretation: Rav and Shmuel's Position on Sanctification After Death

micro_segments.title: Rav and Shmuel's Understanding: Kiddushin Takes Effect Only After the Wife Dies
The Debate Reframed: Sanctifying Something That Has Not Yet Come Into Existence (Davar Shelo Ba L'olam)

macro_segments.title: Rav Adda bar Abba's Alternative: Kiddushin Takes Effect While the Wife Is Alive

micro_segments.title: Rav Adda bar Abba's Position: The Motar Is Sanctified Even During the Wife's Lifetime
The Scenario: When Does the Husband Forfeit His Right to the Excess Labor?

macro_segments.title: The Correspondence Debate: What Exchanges for What (Midi D'Shachich vs. Midi D'Kaitz)

micro_segments.title: First View: Common Things Correspond—Food Bill for Regular Labor, Pin Money for Excess
Second View: Fixed Amounts Correspond—Pin Money for Regular Labor, Variable Food Bill for Excess
The Nafka Mina (Practical Difference): Who Controls What When One Party Refuses

macro_segments.title: Contradictory Sources and Attempts to Reconcile: The Mishnah's Language Examined

micro_segments.title: The Baraita About Pin Money and Its Apparent Support for Fixed-to-Fixed Correspondence
The Gemara's Reinterpretation: How the Mishnah Really Means What Exceeds the Minimum

macro_segments.title: Shmuel's Ruling and the Contradiction with Rabbi Yochanan HaSandlar

micro_segments.title: Shmuel Rules Like Rabbi Yochanan HaSandlar: The Motar Is Not Sanctified
Contradiction Exposed: The Woman's Vow Case (Konam) Versus the Mishnah's Kiddushin

macro_segments.title: The Woman's Vow (Konam) and Delayed Effect: Core Question of Ownership

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah's Woman Who Forbids Her Labor with Konam: Why Can It Take Effect at Divorce?
Rabbi Eliezer's View: An Extraordinary Amount of Labor (Motar Yedei Hadachak) Can Be Hers
Rabbi Yochanan ben Nuri: The Vow Takes Effect Upon Divorce, So Annul It Now

macro_segments.title: Rav Yosef's Explanation: The Unique Power of Konamot Over Hekdesh

micro_segments.title: Konamot Are Different: They Involve the Identity of the Object (Kedusha Shel Guf), Not Mere Ownership Transfer
Examples of Identity Change: Chametz, Freed Slaves, and Korbanot Remove Liens and Liens

macro_segments.title: Abaye's Challenge: Can a Woman Forbid Her Future Labor That She Doesn't Own?

micro_segments.title: Rav Yosef's Initial Analogy: Someone Can Forbid Another's Fruit; Therefore, She Can Forbid Future Labor
Abaye's Objection: You Cannot Make Someone Else's Fruit Forbidden to That Someone Else

macro_segments.title: Rav Huna's Solution: Sanctifying the Hands Themselves (Kedusha Shel Guf)

micro_segments.title: The Hands Exist Now: Reframing What Is Being Sanctified
Objection: Even If the Hands Exist, She Doesn't Own Them—He Does

macro_segments.title: The Delayed Kiddushin Formula: 'When I Get Divorced'

micro_segments.title: The Woman's Declaration: Sanctifying Her Hands for the Period When She Gets Divorced
The Resurfacing Ownership: Base Ownership That Will Return Upon Divorce

macro_segments.title: The Uncertainty Problem: Unlike a 99-Year Lease, Divorce Is Never Guaranteed

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's Concern: The Woman May Die Before Ever Getting Divorced
Rabbi Elai's Analogy: A Sold Field Repurchased (But Still Not Parallel to the Woman's Case)

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yirmiyah's Objection: The Woman Has No Means to Ensure Divorce

micro_segments.title: The Purchaser Controls Repurchase; The Woman Cannot Control Her Husband's Death or Her Own
Rav Sheshet's Reframing: The Woman Still Owns Her Own Body (Unlike a Sold Field)

macro_segments.title: Rav Ashi's Final Framework: Konamot Alter Identity More Fundamentally Than Hekdesh

micro_segments.title: Konamot Work Through Kedusha Shel Guf (Identity) Rather Than Kedusha Shel Damim (Ownership)
The Rabbis Strengthen the Husband's Lien So That Identity Change Doesn't Take Effect During Marriage

macro_segments.title: The Wife's Domestic and Labor Responsibilities: The Mishnah's Enumeration

micro_segments.title: The Core Domestic Duties: Grinding, Baking, Laundry, Cooking, Nursing Children, Making Beds, Spinning
The Effect of Bringing Slaves: Reduction of Duties as the Woman's Labor Obligation Decreases
Four Slaves: The Woman Sits on Her Throne (Complete Exemption)

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer's Dissent: Even with Many Slaves, the Woman Must Spin (Kedusha Shel Guf Perspective)

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer's Rationale: Idleness Leads to Licentiousness (Zeimah)

macro_segments.title: Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel's Alternative View: Idleness Leads to Depression (Shiamum), Not Sin

micro_segments.title: Forced Vow Against the Husband: The Wife's Legal Right to Refuse Labor
The Consequence: Divorce with Ketubah If No Productive Work Is Allowed

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Interpretation of the Mishnah's Labor Descriptions

micro_segments.title: Tochenet (Grinding): Bringing Wheat to the Mill or Hand-Grinding
The Mishnah Is Not Like Rabbi Hiyya's View That a Woman Is Only for Beauty and Children

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Hiyya's Teachings: A Woman's Purpose (Beauty, Children, Adornment) and Practical Implications for Husbands

micro_segments.title: A Woman Is Only for Beauty; A Woman Is Only for Children; A Woman Is Only for Adornment
Practical Advice: Husbands Should Provide Linen Garments and Daughters Should Eat Well for Complexion

topical_tags.term: Davar Shelo Ba L'olam (Thing That Has Not Yet Come Into Existence)
Midi D'Shachich (Common Things Connect to Common Things)
Midi D'Kaitz (Fixed Amounts Connect to Fixed Amounts)
Kedusha Shel Guf (Sanctity of the Object's Identity)
Kedusha Shel Damim (Sanctity of Ownership/Monetary Rights)
